prompt: Our company moved to AWS two years ago but developers still say it feels like the old data center. What might be going wrong?
expected: Skill activates on the canonical Enterprise Non-Cloud symptom and runs the five-characteristic NIST diagnostic (on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service) to identify which enterprise controls have cancelled cloud properties, then names each violating mechanism and its cloud-operating-model remedy.

prompt: Our cloud provisioning process takes three weeks because every new environment needs security approval. Is this normal?
expected: Skill activates and identifies the manual approval gate as a direct violation of on-demand self-service — one of the five Enterprise Non-Cloud failure modes — and prescribes the cloud-operating-model alternative: a self-service portal with pre-approved configurations and policy-as-code guardrails that satisfies security without multi-week ticketing.

prompt: Our central IT team charges every application team a flat $10,000/month for 'cloud platform support' regardless of how much they use. Is this a problem?
expected: Skill activates and names the fixed overhead charge as a violation of measured service that negates the cloud's consumption-based value proposition, recommending replacement with consumption-based internal billing (showback/chargeback with real-time cost dashboards) that still satisfies the finance cost-allocation requirement.

prompt: We're building an internal cloud platform for our enterprise. How do we evaluate whether what we build will actually qualify as a cloud?
expected: Skill activates and prescribes running the five NIST characteristics as a pre-build self-assessment against the proposed platform design, scoring how many are substantially satisfied and setting measurable targets per characteristic so gaps are caught before the platform becomes an Enterprise Non-Cloud.

prompt: Finance requires that each team have their own dedicated cloud account with reserved instances so we can track costs accurately. Is there a better approach?
expected: Skill activates and explains that dedicated instances for cost tracking violate resource pooling; it provides the cloud-native alternative — cost allocation tags with showback/chargeback on shared, pooled resources — which delivers the accurate cost tracking finance wants without abandoning the shared multi-tenant pool.

prompt: A vendor is selling us an 'enterprise cloud solution' that routes all traffic through our data center for security monitoring. Should we buy it?
expected: Skill activates and uses the NIST diagnostic to evaluate the vendor pitch, identifying that routing all traffic through the corporate data center violates broad network access, and recommends a zero-trust, identity-based access architecture as the cloud-native way to satisfy the security-monitoring requirement instead of buying the data-center-proxy product.

prompt: We need to decide whether to run our workloads on AWS, Azure, or GCP. Which should we pick?
Our cloud costs went up 40% last quarter and nobody knows why. How do we get visibility?
We're 70% through our cloud migration but the business doesn't see value. What's happening?
We want to establish Kubernetes as our standard container platform. How should we write this as a guiding principle?
We're in financial services and regulators require all production data to stay in a specific geographic region and be accessible only to authorized staff. Does this mean we can't use real cloud?
expected: Skill applies the diagnostic to pinpoint which NIST characteristics the regulation actually touches, but per the boundary section recognizes some compliance constraints are non-negotiable; the defensible answer is not 'remove the constraint' but satisfy it with cloud-native patterns (region pinning, identity-based access) where possible and accept the limitation only for the specific affected workloads.

prompt: Our legacy ERP system was 'lifted and shifted' to the cloud. It's on AWS but it's a single VM with no auto-scaling and takes a week to patch. Is this a cloud migration?
expected: Skill applies the diagnostic and shows rapid elasticity (and self-service patching) is violated, so this is an Enterprise Non-Cloud outcome for that workload; but because a legacy ERP may genuinely not benefit from all cloud characteristics, the defensible answer is explicit segmentation — treat this app separately rather than forcing full remediation — while maximizing genuine cloud characteristics across the rest of the portfolio.
